lesson: L09 — Area of Parallelograms, Triangles & Trapezoids
banner: Week 10 · Prove It — Area of Parallelograms, Triangles & Trapezoids
essential_question: Why is a triangle's area always exactly half of a same-base-same-height rectangle, and why does a trapezoid need the AVERAGE of its two bases instead of just one?
objective: Justify why a triangle's area is half of a rectangle sharing its base and height, and why a trapezoid's area uses the average of its two bases, review the week's Math-U-See practice (Lesson Practice 9A & 9B), and pass the mastery checkpoint on parallelogram, triangle, and trapezoid area.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a parallelogram, a triangle, and a trapezoid, and mark a believable base and a perpendicular height on each — including the trapezoid's second base. Then say out loud, in your own words, what makes a height 'the height' instead of just any slanted side.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 9A & 9B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 9A/9B. We check the tricky ones: telling the true perpendicular height apart from a slanted side, remembering to average the trapezoid's two bases before multiplying, and the fill-in-the-blank sentences about parallelograms, triangles, and quadrilateral heights. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: a parallelogram's area is base × height — the same rule as a rectangle. Argue it: if you cut a triangular sliver off one slanted end of a parallelogram and slide it to the other end, what shape do you get, and why doesn't its area change?

Claim 2: a triangle's area is always exactly HALF of a rectangle sharing the same base and height — never a third, never two-thirds. Argue it: picture two identical copies of the same triangle, one flipped and fitted against the other along the slanted side — what shape do the two copies make together, and what does that prove about one triangle alone?

Claim 3: a trapezoid's area uses the AVERAGE of its two parallel bases, not just one of them. Argue it: picture two identical copies of the same trapezoid, one flipped upside-down and slid up against the other — what parallelogram do the two copies form, and how does its base relate to the trapezoid's two original bases?

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 10 — Classifying Triangles by Sides & Angles — where these shapes get sorted into families like scalene, isosceles, equilateral, acute, right, and obtuse.

success_criteria.text: I can state and use the area formulas for a parallelogram, a triangle, and a trapezoid without help
I can justify why a triangle's area is half of a same-base-same-height rectangle and why a trapezoid uses the average of its two bases
I can find the area of a composite figure and correctly measure a real shape's base and perpendicular height

questions.prompt: A parallelogram has a base of 11 cm and a height of 6 cm. Its area is…
The area of a triangle is ___ the area of a rectangle with the same base and height.
questions.explain: Math-U-See's own rule: the area of a triangle is one-half the area of a rectangle with the same base and height — never equal to it or twice it.

questions.prompt: A trapezoid has bases of 7 in and 13 in and a height of 4 in. Find its area, showing the average-base step.
questions.explain: Average the two bases first, then multiply by the height — skipping the averaging step (and just using one base) is the single most common trapezoid mistake.

questions.prompt: Measure the base and the true PERPENDICULAR height (not the slanted side) of a real triangular object near you right now (a slice of bread, a ramp, a roof gable seen from the side, a folded napkin) and compute its area.
questions.answer: Any honest measured base and height with units and a correctly computed A = ½·b·h, e.g. a folded-napkin triangle b = 18 cm, h = 12 cm → A = ½ × 18 × 12 = 108 cm².
questions.explain: Rubric: base and height measured and recorded with units, height genuinely perpendicular to the base (1 pt) + area correctly computed as ½·b·h from those numbers (1 pt).

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw a composite figure made of a rectangle and a triangle stacked together, label every dimension, and compute its total area by splitting it into the two simpler shapes and adding.
questions.answer: Any correctly labeled composite whose two parts are each computed correctly and summed, e.g. rectangle 8×4 = 32 + triangle ½×8×3 = 12 → total 44 sq units.
questions.explain: Rubric: two clearly labeled shapes with all dimensions marked (1 pt) + each part's area computed correctly and summed to a correct total (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why two identical copies of the same triangle — one flipped and fitted against the other along the slanted side — always form a parallelogram, and what that proves about a single triangle's area.
questions.answer: Two congruent copies of a triangle fit together edge-to-edge to form a parallelogram with the same base and the same height as one triangle; since that parallelogram's area is base × height and it's made of exactly two copies of the triangle, one triangle must be exactly half of base × height.
questions.explain: Full credit names the two-copies-make-a-parallelogram argument and connects it directly to why one triangle is exactly half of base × height — not an estimate, an exact half.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 9 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
